options.C: declining depreciation expense reducing non-cash charges

questions.explanation: If revenue grows much faster than operating cash flows, receivables are likely ballooning — the firm may be recognising revenue before cash is collected. This is a classic earnings quality red flag (aggressive accrual accounting).

questions.subject: Financial Statement Analysis
questions.question: When projecting a firm's capital expenditure for a financial model, the most analytically defensible approach is to:

options.A: hold capex constant at the most recent year's level to be conservative
options.B: estimate capex as a percentage of revenue based on historical norms and management guidance
options.C: exclude maintenance capex and only model growth capex

questions.explanation: Modelling capex as a percentage of revenue (calibrated to historical patterns and management guidance) ties investment to growth drivers. Holding capex flat ignores scale effects; excluding maintenance capex understates true capital intensity.

questions.subject: Financial Statement Analysis
questions.question: Which of the following items is typically included in other comprehensive income (OCI) rather than net income?

options.A: Gain on sale of a discontinued operation
options.B: Unrealised gain on available-for-sale (AFS) securities under US GAAP (pre-ASC 321 update)
options.C: Loss from early extinguishment of debt

questions.explanation: Under legacy US GAAP, unrealised gains on AFS securities bypass the income statement and go to OCI. Gains on discontinued operations and debt extinguishment losses flow through the income statement (net income).

questions.subject: Financial Statement Analysis
questions.question: Which of the following is most likely a red flag indicating earnings manipulation through channel stuffing?

options.A: Rising receivable days with revenue growth concentrated at quarter-end
options.B: Stable operating margins with low revenue variability
options.C: Declining inventory levels relative to cost of goods sold

questions.explanation: Channel stuffing (shipping excess products to distributors near period-end to inflate revenue) causes receivables to spike, increasing DSO. Revenue recognised in Q4 with large receivables and subsequent returns/write-offs are classic warning signs.

questions.subject: Financial Statement Analysis
questions.question: A firm's interest coverage ratio (ICR = EBIT/Interest) has fallen from 8x to 2x over three years. The most appropriate conclusion is:

options.A: the firm is in immediate risk of insolvency
options.B: the firm's debt service capacity has weakened significantly and warrants credit quality reassessment
options.C: the firm has optimised its capital structure by increasing financial leverage

questions.explanation: ICR of 2x means EBIT covers interest only twice — thin margin of safety. This weakening trend signals deteriorating debt service capacity and increased financial risk. Immediate insolvency is not certain (it depends on cash flows and maturities), but credit risk is elevated.

questions.subject: Financial Statement Analysis
questions.question: A firm switches from straight-line depreciation to double-declining balance depreciation. In the early years of an asset's life, this change will most likely:

options.A: increase reported net income and reduce the asset's book value
options.B: reduce reported net income and reduce the asset's book value
options.C: reduce net income but have no impact on asset book value

questions.explanation: Double-declining balance produces higher depreciation in early years → lower net income and lower book value of the asset. The effect reverses in later years (double-declining depreciation decreases over time).

questions.subject: Financial Statement Analysis
questions.topic: IFRS vs US GAAP – Key Differences
questions.question: A key difference between IFRS and US GAAP regarding inventory is that:

options.A: US GAAP allows the LIFO inventory method while IFRS prohibits it
options.B: IFRS requires inventory to be measured at the higher of cost or net realisable value
options.C: US GAAP requires reversal of inventory write-downs when NRV recovers

questions.explanation: LIFO is permitted under US GAAP but prohibited under IFRS (IAS 2). IFRS requires NRV write-downs when NRV < cost, and allows reversal when NRV subsequently recovers — US GAAP does not allow write-down reversals.

questions.subject: Financial Statement Analysis
questions.question: For bank analysis, the most important profitability metric is typically:

options.A: Return on Assets (ROA), because banks are asset-intensive businesses
options.B: Net Interest Margin (NIM), reflecting the spread between lending and borrowing rates
options.C: Operating margin, because banks have significant fixed operating costs

questions.explanation: NIM (Net Interest Income / Earning Assets) is the most critical profitability metric for banks because it captures the core revenue-generating spread between what banks earn on loans and pay on deposits. ROA and ROE are also standard, but NIM is the primary driver.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A sell-side analyst reduces her rating on a company after management threatens to revoke access if she publishes a negative report. This most directly violates:

options.A: Standard V(A) – Diligence and Reasonable Basis
options.B: Standard I(B) – Independence and Objectivity
options.C: Standard II(B) – Market Manipulation

questions.explanation: Changing recommendations due to management pressure — the threat of losing access — compromises the analyst's independence and objectivity, violating Standard I(B). Maintaining independence from issuer pressure is a core requirement for sell-side analysts.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: Best execution under the CFA Standards requires a manager to:

options.A: always trade with the broker offering the lowest commission
options.B: use reasonable care to maximise the value of the client portfolio, considering execution quality, cost, speed, and other relevant factors
options.C: document that every trade was executed at the national best bid and offer (NBBO)

questions.explanation: Best execution is a multi-dimensional concept: it considers price, transaction costs, speed of execution, likelihood of execution, and other factors. Lowest commission alone does not constitute best execution if other factors are compromised.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A portfolio manager issues a research note that includes a price target but omits the key assumptions and valuation model underlying the target. This most likely violates:

options.A: Standard V(B) – Communication with Clients, by omitting material information needed to understand the recommendation
options.B: Standard I(C) – Misrepresentation, by presenting an unsupported conclusion
options.C: Standard III(B) – Fair Dealing, by not communicating equally to all clients

questions.explanation: Standard V(B) requires that communications include the basic characteristics of the investment, key assumptions, and limitations. A price target without supporting assumptions fails to give clients the context needed to evaluate the recommendation.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A portfolio manager negotiates a performance fee of 20% of returns above a hurdle rate with a new client. Her employer is unaware of this arrangement. She most likely violates:

options.A: Standard VI(A) – Disclosure of Conflicts, because performance fees create incentives to take excessive risk
options.B: Standard IV(B) – Additional Compensation Arrangements, because she must disclose and obtain employer consent
options.C: Standard III(C) – Suitability, because the fee structure may not be appropriate for the client

questions.explanation: Standard IV(B) requires written consent from the employer before accepting additional compensation that could create a conflict with the employer's interests. Failing to disclose the arrangement violates this standard regardless of whether the fee is fair to the client.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A candidate for the CFA exam uses an unofficial third-party summary of CFA Institute curriculum material as her primary study resource. She most likely:

options.A: violates Standard VII(A) because only official CFA materials may be used for exam preparation
options.B: does not violate any CFA Standard because the choice of study materials is the candidate's discretion
options.C: violates Standard I(C) if she presents knowledge derived from unofficial materials as her own

questions.explanation: The CFA Standards do not mandate use of official materials only — candidates may use any study aids. Standard VII(A) governs conduct during the exam and in the profession. Standard I(C) would apply if she misrepresented her qualifications, not merely the source of her preparation.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A portfolio manager does not vote proxies because it requires time and resources. This most likely violates:

options.A: Standard III(A) – Loyalty, Prudence, and Care, because proxy voting is part of the duty to clients
options.B: Standard III(B) – Fair Dealing, because not all clients receive equal representation
options.C: Standard VI(A) – Disclosure of Conflicts

questions.explanation: Standard III(A) requires managers to vote proxies in the best interest of clients. Failing to vote proxies because it is inconvenient does not fulfil the duty of loyalty and care owed to clients, as proxies are a valuable right that affects portfolio company governance.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A company selectively discloses material non-public information to a favoured analyst before the public announcement. The analyst who receives and trades on this information violates:

options.A: Standard II(A) – Material Non-Public Information
options.B: Standard I(A) – Knowledge of the Law, because Regulation FD (in the US) prohibits selective disclosure
options.C: Both Standard II(A) and potentially Standard I(A) if Reg FD applies

questions.explanation: Trading on selectively disclosed MNPI violates Standard II(A). If the analyst is operating in a US context, Regulation FD also prohibits selective disclosure, making Standard I(A) potentially relevant as well. Both standards are implicated.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A member discovers that her firm is defrauding clients by misappropriating funds. After internal escalation fails, she should:

options.A: continue working at the firm while refusing personal involvement in the fraud
options.B: dissociate from the fraud and consider reporting to regulatory authorities
options.C: disclose the fraud publicly on social media to protect investors

questions.explanation: Standard I(A) and IV(A) require that members not participate in or facilitate illegal conduct. After failed internal resolution, dissociation from the activity is mandatory. Reporting to regulators may be required by law and is consistent with Standards. Public social media disclosure is not the appropriate channel.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A quantitative analyst develops a model that generates buy/sell signals. She publishes the signals without disclosing the model's assumptions or limitations. This most likely violates:

options.A: Standard V(B) – Communication with Clients, by failing to disclose material limitations of the model
options.B: Standard V(A) – Diligence, because quantitative models require less disclosure than fundamental research
options.C: Standard I(C) – Misrepresentation, only if the model's track record is fabricated

questions.explanation: Standard V(B) requires that recommendations include sufficient information about the investment process, assumptions, and limitations to allow clients to evaluate the recommendation. Model-based signals without disclosure of key assumptions violate this standard.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: An analyst's firm has a banking relationship with a company. She is about to issue a sell rating. Her manager pressures her to change it to a hold. She should:

options.A: change the rating to avoid conflict with her employer
options.B: maintain the sell rating, support it with a reasonable basis, and document the pressure received
options.C: remove herself from coverage of the company and let another analyst issue a report

questions.explanation: Standard I(B) requires independence. The analyst must maintain her justified rating. She should document the pressure (for regulatory protection) and may escalate. If she cannot maintain independence, withdrawal from coverage may be appropriate — but changing the rating due to pressure is a direct violation.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: The pure expectations theory of the term structure implies that:

options.A: long-term rates reflect a liquidity premium over expected future short rates
options.B: forward rates are unbiased predictors of expected future spot rates
options.C: the yield curve is always upward sloping because of the liquidity preference premium

questions.explanation: Pure expectations theory: long-term rates are geometric averages of expected future short rates. Forward rates are unbiased estimates of future spot rates. Liquidity preference theory adds a premium, explaining the typical upward slope.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: The credit spread of a corporate bond reflects compensation for:

options.A: interest rate risk and reinvestment risk only
options.B: default risk, liquidity risk, and tax disadvantages relative to government bonds
options.C: inflation risk and call option risk embedded in corporate bonds

questions.explanation: Corporate bond credit spreads compensate investors for: (1) default/credit risk, (2) liquidity risk (corporates trade less frequently than govts), and (3) in some jurisdictions, tax treatment differences. Duration-related interest rate risk is not captured by the spread.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A bond has modified duration of 7 and convexity of 80. For a 100bps increase in yield, the approximate price change is:
questions.explanation: ΔP/P ≈ −D_mod × Δy + 0.5 × Convexity × (Δy)² = −7 × 0.01 + 0.5 × 80 × (0.01)² = −0.07 + 0.004 = −0.066 = −6.6%. Convexity always adds value (reduces the loss) for rate increases.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: In a mortgage-backed security (MBS), prepayment risk refers to:

options.A: the risk that mortgagors default on their loans, reducing principal payments
options.B: the risk that mortgagors repay principal faster than expected, reducing the investor's reinvestment income
options.C: the risk that the servicer fails to pass through principal payments to MBS investors

questions.explanation: Prepayment risk is the risk of early principal return. When rates fall, mortgagors refinance (prepay), returning capital to MBS investors who must reinvest at lower rates (reinvestment risk). Default is credit risk; servicer failure is operational risk.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: The OAS (option-adjusted spread) of a callable bond is:

options.A: always higher than the Z-spread because of the call option premium
options.B: the Z-spread minus the value of the embedded call option (in spread terms)
options.C: equal to the nominal spread because option-adjusted analysis removes spread distortions

questions.explanation: Z-spread = OAS + option cost (for a callable bond). The issuer holds the call, so the investor is short the call. OAS removes the option's value to isolate the pure credit/liquidity spread. For putable bonds, OAS > Z-spread.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A floating rate note (FRN) with a semi-annual reset will have its price closest to par when:

options.A: the credit spread of the issuer has widened since issuance
options.B: the reference rate and credit spread are unchanged from the last reset date
options.C: market interest rates have risen significantly since the last reset

questions.explanation: An FRN resets its coupon each period to the reference rate + spread. If both remain unchanged, the next coupon will equal the market required return and the bond prices at par. Spread widening reduces the FRN price below par.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: An investor buys protection in a credit default swap (CDS). If the reference entity defaults, the investor receives:

options.A: the CDS spread payments accumulated to that date
options.B: the par value of the bond minus its recovery value (loss given default)
options.C: the market value of the reference bond at the time of default

questions.explanation: In a physical settlement CDS, the protection buyer delivers the defaulted bond and receives par. In cash settlement, the protection buyer receives par − recovery value = loss given default. The CDS spread is what the buyer pays (not receives).

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A bond manager who believes the yield curve will flatten (long rates fall relative to short rates) should:

options.A: position the portfolio with a barbell — overweight in short and long maturities
options.B: overweight long-duration bonds and underweight short-duration bonds
options.C: position in a bullet portfolio concentrated at intermediate maturities

questions.explanation: If long rates are expected to fall relative to short rates (curve flattening), long-duration bonds will appreciate more than short bonds. A manager should increase long-bond exposure. A barbell benefits from non-parallel shifts; a bullet is a neutral structure.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: Given: 1-year spot rate = 4%, 2-year par rate = 5%. The 2-year spot rate is closest to:

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: Covered bonds differ from asset-backed securities (ABS) primarily because:

options.A: covered bonds are issued by special purpose vehicles while ABS remain on the issuer's balance sheet
options.B: covered bond investors have dual recourse — to the cover pool and to the issuing bank
options.C: ABS provide full recourse to the originating institution in addition to the asset pool

questions.explanation: Covered bonds remain on the issuer's balance sheet; if the cover pool is insufficient, investors have recourse to the full balance sheet of the issuing bank (dual recourse). ABS are typically off-balance-sheet true sales with no recourse to the originator.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: Key rate duration (KRD) differs from modified duration in that KRD:

options.A: measures price sensitivity to a parallel shift in the entire yield curve
options.B: measures price sensitivity to a change in yield at a specific maturity point on the curve
options.C: incorporates convexity to improve the accuracy of price change estimates

questions.explanation: KRDs decompose a bond's total interest rate sensitivity across specific points on the yield curve. They are useful for non-parallel shift analysis. Modified duration captures parallel shift sensitivity; convexity is a separate adjustment.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A sovereign that issues debt in its own currency is least likely to default because:

options.A: it can monetise the debt by printing money, though this risks hyperinflation
options.B: multilateral institutions (IMF/World Bank) guarantee all sovereign domestic currency debt
options.C: domestic currency debt always has lower interest rates than foreign currency debt

questions.explanation: A sovereign that controls its own currency can theoretically always repay domestic currency debt by creating money (monetisation), but at the cost of inflation. This is why technical default on local-currency sovereign debt is rare (Argentina/Zimbabwe inflated rather than formally defaulted).

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: An analyst finds that a 10-year corporate bond's OAS is 150bps while similar issuers trade at 120bps OAS. The bond appears to be:

options.A: overvalued because its OAS is higher than peers
options.B: undervalued because investors receive more compensation for credit risk than comparable bonds
options.C: fairly valued because OAS differences reflect real differences in credit quality

questions.explanation: Higher OAS on a comparable credit means greater compensation for risk — the bond offers more spread per unit of credit risk and appears undervalued. If OAS converges to peers, the price will rise, generating a positive return.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: In a collateralised mortgage obligation (CMO), planned amortisation class (PAC) tranches provide:

options.A: the highest prepayment risk because they receive all prepayments first
options.B: stable cash flows within a specified prepayment band by shifting prepayment risk to support (companion) tranches
options.C: guaranteed principal repayment regardless of mortgage pool prepayment rates

questions.explanation: PAC tranches receive predictable principal cash flows within a PSA band. Support/companion tranches absorb prepayment variability (both fast and slow prepayments). PAC investors sacrifice yield for cash flow stability; companion investors receive higher yields for absorbing risk.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A portfolio manager wants to reduce the portfolio's duration from 8 to 5 years using interest rate futures. She should:

options.A: buy Treasury bond futures contracts
options.B: sell Treasury bond futures contracts
options.C: enter into a receive-fixed interest rate swap

questions.explanation: Selling futures reduces duration (short futures profits when rates rise — hedges against rising rate risk). Buying futures increases duration. A receive-fixed swap also increases duration (fixed-rate receiver position = long duration).

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: When analysing high-yield (speculative grade) bonds, the most important analytical focus shifts from:

options.A: leverage ratios to earnings quality metrics
options.B: repayment capacity and enterprise value to default probability and recovery rates
options.C: yield spreads to absolute yield levels and current income

questions.explanation: For investment-grade bonds, default is rare and spread analysis dominates. For high-yield bonds, analysts focus on recovery analysis (what's the bond worth in default?) and liquidity/enterprise value relative to debt. Default and recovery are first-order concerns.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: Under liability-driven investing (LDI), a pension fund primarily aims to:

options.A: maximise total return on the asset portfolio to generate surplus
options.B: match the duration and cash flow profile of assets to the duration of plan liabilities
options.C: hold only risk-free government bonds to eliminate credit risk entirely

questions.explanation: LDI focuses on hedging interest rate risk on plan liabilities. By matching asset duration to liability duration, the fund reduces surplus volatility — when rates fall and liabilities rise, assets rise proportionately, preserving the funding ratio.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: An affirmative covenant in a bond indenture most likely requires the issuer to:

options.A: refrain from issuing additional debt above a specified leverage ratio
options.B: maintain insurance on assets and comply with all applicable laws
options.C: avoid paying dividends above a maximum payout threshold

questions.explanation: Affirmative (positive) covenants require the issuer to do something — maintain insurance, deliver financial statements, preserve assets. Negative covenants restrict actions — limiting new debt, dividends, asset sales. B is an affirmative covenant; A and C are negative covenants.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: The G-spread of a corporate bond is defined as:

options.A: the spread over the benchmark government bond yield of comparable maturity
options.B: the constant spread added to all points on the government spot curve to equate the bond's discounted cash flows to its market price
options.C: the spread that makes the option-adjusted value equal to the market price

questions.explanation: The G-spread is the simple yield difference between the corporate bond and the nearest on-the-run government bond. The Z-spread uses the entire spot curve (answer B). The OAS adjusts for embedded options (answer C).

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: The return of a fixed income portfolio over a period can be decomposed into which primary components?

options.A: Coupon income, price effect from yield change (duration effect), and rolldown return
options.B: Current yield, capital gain/loss, and reinvestment income on reinvested coupons
options.C: Both A and B capture overlapping decomposition frameworks used in attribution

questions.explanation: Fixed income return attribution uses various frameworks. The Campisi model includes: coupon return, price return from yield changes (duration × yield change), rolldown (pull to par), and spread return. B's decomposition is also valid. Both frameworks are used in practice.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: Agency MBS issued by Ginnie Mae (GNMA) differ from Fannie Mae (FNMA) MBS in that:

options.A: GNMA carries the full faith and credit guarantee of the US government; FNMA carries only an implicit guarantee
options.B: GNMA securities are backed by conventional mortgages while FNMA backs FHA/VA loans
options.C: FNMA MBS are exempt from all prepayment risk while GNMA MBS carry prepayment risk

questions.explanation: GNMA is a government agency with an explicit US government guarantee (full faith and credit). FNMA and FHLMC are government-sponsored enterprises (GSEs) that were implicitly (now effectively explicitly) backed after the 2008 conservatorship. All agency MBS carry prepayment risk.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: For TIPS (Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities), the principal is indexed to inflation. If inflation rises, the coupon payment:

options.A: remains fixed in dollar terms because the coupon rate is fixed
options.B: rises in dollar terms because it is applied to the inflation-adjusted (higher) principal
options.C: falls in real terms, compensating investors through principal accretion only

questions.explanation: TIPS coupon payments = fixed coupon rate × inflation-adjusted principal. As inflation rises, the principal accretes, and the next coupon (fixed rate × higher principal) rises in nominal dollar terms. The investor thus receives higher nominal coupon payments.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A credit rating transition matrix shows a BBB-rated bond has a 5% probability of being downgraded to BB in one year. An investor using this information for portfolio management is most concerned about:

options.A: the bond's probability of outright default within one year
options.B: mark-to-market losses from spread widening upon downgrade to high-yield
options.C: the bond's coupon payments being reduced upon downgrade

questions.explanation: A fallen angel (investment-grade to high-yield downgrade) forces institutional investors who cannot hold sub-investment-grade bonds to sell, widening spreads and causing mark-to-market losses. Coupon terms don't change on downgrade; default probability is a different risk.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: In an arbitrage-free binomial interest rate tree, the value of a bond is computed by:

options.A: discounting all cash flows at the bond's yield to maturity
options.B: backward induction — discounting expected future values and coupons at each node's one-period rate
options.C: computing the present value of cash flows using the Z-spread

questions.explanation: The binomial tree model uses backward induction: at each terminal node, cash flows are known. Moving backward, each node's value = (0.5×V_up + 0.5×V_down + coupon) / (1 + r_node). This ensures arbitrage-free pricing consistent with the observed yield curve.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A portfolio with positive convexity will outperform a duration-matched portfolio with lower convexity when:

options.A: yields remain unchanged (flat curve environment)
options.B: yields experience large moves in either direction
options.C: the yield curve undergoes only a parallel downward shift

questions.explanation: Convexity benefits materialise on large rate moves — the price appreciation on a rate decline exceeds the price loss on an equal rate rise. In a stable rate environment, you pay for convexity (via lower yield) without benefiting.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: All else equal, when interest rate volatility increases, the value of a putable bond will:

options.A: decrease, because higher volatility increases the option cost borne by the investor
options.B: increase, because the embedded put option becomes more valuable to the investor
options.C: remain unchanged, because the put option value is insensitive to volatility in fixed income markets

questions.explanation: A putable bond gives the investor the right to sell the bond back to the issuer at a set price. Like all options, the put option's value increases with volatility — greater rate uncertainty raises the probability of the put being exercised profitably. Therefore: Value of putable bond = Value of straight bond + Value of put option. Higher volatility → higher put value → higher putable bond value. Contrast with callable bonds, where higher volatility reduces value to the investor.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A convertible bond has a conversion ratio of 25 shares per bond and the underlying stock trades at $38. The conversion value and straight (investment) value are $950 and $880 respectively. The minimum value (floor value) of the convertible bond is:
questions.explanation: Conversion value = conversion ratio × stock price = 25 × $38 = $950. The minimum (floor) value of a convertible bond is the greater of (1) conversion value and (2) straight bond value. max($950, $880) = $950. The convertible trades at a premium above this floor due to the option value embedded in the conversion feature.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: Spread duration measures the sensitivity of a bond's price to changes in:

options.A: the risk-free benchmark yield, holding the credit spread constant
options.B: the bond's credit spread, holding the risk-free rate constant
options.C: the bond's yield to maturity, incorporating both benchmark rate and spread changes simultaneously

questions.explanation: Spread duration measures how much a bond's price changes for a 1 basis point change in its credit (OAS) spread, with the risk-free benchmark rate held constant. It isolates credit risk from interest rate risk. A bond with a spread duration of 5 will lose approximately 5% in price if its credit spread widens by 100 bps.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A portfolio manager executes a butterfly trade by going long intermediate-maturity bonds and short equal-duration-weighted positions in short- and long-maturity bonds. This trade profits when:

options.A: the yield curve experiences a parallel upward shift
options.B: the yield curve humps — intermediate yields fall relative to short and long yields (curve bulges in the middle)
options.C: the yield curve flattens with long rates declining relative to short rates

questions.explanation: A long butterfly (long body, short wings) profits when the yield curve becomes more humped — that is, intermediate yields decline relative to short- and long-maturity yields. The intermediate bonds (long position) appreciate while the short/long wings (short positions) decline in price. This is a non-parallel shift trade. A short butterfly (short body, long wings) profits from curve flattening.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.topic: Credit Analysis – IG vs HY Metrics
questions.question: For investment-grade corporate issuers, the most important credit metric is generally:

options.A: asset coverage ratio — the ratio of liquidation value to total debt outstanding
options.B: interest coverage ratio — EBITDA divided by interest expense — reflecting ability to service debt from earnings
options.C: the equity cushion — market capitalisation relative to total debt, reflecting residual enterprise value

questions.explanation: Investment-grade issuers are evaluated primarily on their capacity to service debt from ongoing cash flows. Interest coverage (EBITDA/Interest) and leverage (Debt/EBITDA) ratios are the primary metrics. Asset coverage and liquidation analysis (answer A) become more relevant for distressed/high-yield issuers where recovery analysis dominates.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A 6×9 FRA was entered at a fixed rate of 4.5%. Three months later, the 3-month LIBOR has risen to 5.2%. The FRA has:

options.A: negative value to the long (fixed-rate receiver) because rates have risen above the contract rate
options.B: positive value to the long (fixed-rate payer) because rates have risen above the FRA rate
options.C: zero value because FRA gains and losses are only settled at the end of the underlying loan period

questions.explanation: In an FRA, the 'long' position is the party that agrees to pay the fixed (FRA) rate and receive the floating (LIBOR) rate. A 6×9 FRA is a 3-month loan starting in 6 months. If LIBOR rises above the FRA rate (5.2% > 4.5%), the long benefits — they locked in a lower fixed borrowing rate. The FRA has positive mark-to-market value to the fixed payer (long).

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: The weighted average life (WAL) of a mortgage-backed security is a useful measure because:

options.A: it equals the Macaulay duration of the MBS and can be used for interest rate hedging directly
options.B: it provides an estimate of the expected time until principal repayment, accounting for prepayment assumptions
options.C: it measures the present value-weighted timing of all cash flows including coupon and principal payments

questions.explanation: WAL is the principal-weighted average time to receipt of principal payments (both scheduled amortisation and prepayments). It is not the same as Macaulay duration (which weights all cash flows by PV). WAL helps investors compare MBS with different prepayment profiles and is sensitive to the assumed prepayment speed (PSA).

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: External credit enhancement for an ABS transaction differs from internal credit enhancement in that external enhancement:

options.A: relies on the structural subordination of junior tranches to protect senior tranche holders
options.B: uses third-party guarantees or letters of credit from a financially strong external institution
options.C: creates overcollateralisation by including more assets in the pool than required to support the notes

questions.explanation: External credit enhancements introduce a third party's credit quality — such as a bank letter of credit, monoline insurance wrap, or government guarantee. Internal enhancements use structural features: subordination (A), overcollateralisation (C), and excess spread. External enhancement exposes investors to third-party credit risk (e.g., insurer downgrade).

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: In a repurchase agreement (repo), the repo rate is most accurately described as:

options.A: the coupon rate on the collateral security used in the repo transaction
options.B: the implied interest rate on the short-term loan, calculated as the difference between the repurchase price and the sale price
options.C: the overnight rate set by the central bank that the repo rate must equal under regulatory rules

questions.explanation: In a repo, the dealer sells a security (e.g., Treasury) and agrees to repurchase it at a higher price. The difference between the repurchase price and the initial sale price represents the interest cost — the repo rate. It is a collateralised borrowing rate, typically near but not equal to the policy rate. Higher-quality collateral commands lower repo rates.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: Effective duration is preferred over modified duration for bonds with embedded options because:

options.A: effective duration uses coupon cash flows adjusted for reinvestment, making it more accurate
options.B: effective duration accounts for the change in cash flows when yields change, as the option's exercise affects timing of payments
options.C: modified duration overstates interest rate sensitivity because it ignores convexity adjustments

questions.explanation: Modified duration assumes cash flows are fixed regardless of yield changes. For callable or putable bonds, cash flows themselves change when yields change (a call is more likely to be exercised when rates fall, shortening the effective maturity). Effective duration is computed numerically — using actual bond prices at higher and lower yield scenarios — to capture this cash flow optionality.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: The Vasicek interest rate model is characterised by mean reversion. A key limitation of this model is that:

options.A: it does not incorporate the possibility of negative interest rates
options.B: it allows interest rates to become negative, which was historically considered unrealistic
options.C: it uses a multi-factor structure that is difficult to calibrate to observed yield curves

questions.explanation: The Vasicek model is a single-factor mean-reverting model: dr = κ(θ−r)dt + σdW. A known limitation is that rates can theoretically go negative (as the model allows r to cross zero). In the post-2008/2015 era of negative rates in Europe and Japan, this is less of a theoretical flaw, but it was historically criticised. The Cox-Ingersoll-Ross (CIR) model addresses this by making volatility proportional to √r, preventing negative rates.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: Bonds issued by US government-sponsored enterprises (GSEs) such as Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac carry:

options.A: zero credit risk because they are backed by the full faith and credit of the US government
options.B: an implicit (and post-2008 effective) government support, but technically no explicit legal guarantee
options.C: credit risk equal to that of the underlying mortgage pools because GSE bonds are pass-through certificates

questions.explanation: GSE bonds (FNMA, FHLMC) are not legally guaranteed by the US Treasury — they carry only an implicit guarantee based on their government-sponsored status. Since 2008, FNMA and FHLMC have been in federal conservatorship, effectively making their obligations government-backed in practice. Only GNMA (Ginnie Mae) carries an explicit full faith and credit guarantee.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: When immunising a bond portfolio against multiple future liabilities, which condition is necessary in addition to matching the portfolio's duration to the liability duration?

options.A: The portfolio's convexity must be less than the liability's convexity to minimise reinvestment risk
options.B: The portfolio must contain only zero-coupon bonds maturing exactly at each liability date
options.C: The present value of portfolio assets must equal the present value of liabilities, and asset convexity should equal or exceed liability convexity

questions.explanation: Multiple-liability immunisation requires: (1) PV(assets) = PV(liabilities), (2) Duration(assets) = Duration(liabilities), and (3) Convexity(assets) ≥ Convexity(liabilities). Excess asset convexity provides a cushion against non-parallel rate shifts. Zero-coupon bond portfolios are sufficient for single liabilities but may not be cost-efficient for multiple liabilities.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: Commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) differ from residential MBS in that CMBS loans are typically:

options.A: fully amortising over a 30-year period with no balloon payment risk
options.B: partially amortising with large balloon payments at maturity, creating refinancing (extension) risk
options.C: floating-rate instruments with no prepayment penalties, making prepayment analysis less important

questions.explanation: CMBS loans typically have 5–10 year terms with amortisation schedules based on a 25–30 year horizon — resulting in a large balloon payment at maturity. If the borrower cannot refinance, the loan defaults. This 'balloon risk' is a key credit concern in CMBS analysis. CMBS often also include prepayment lockouts and yield maintenance provisions, making prepayment risk lower (not absent).

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A bond's roll-down return (also called rolldown return or riding the yield curve) is positive when:

options.A: the yield curve is inverted and the bond's yield rises as it approaches maturity
options.B: the yield curve is upward sloping and the bond's yield falls as it 'rolls down' to shorter maturities over time
options.C: the bond's coupon rate is higher than its current yield to maturity

questions.explanation: Roll-down return arises from the passage of time on an upward-sloping yield curve. As a bond's maturity shortens over time, its yield declines (it rolls to a lower point on the curve), generating price appreciation. Holding a 10-year bond for a year and selling it as a 9-year bond at a lower yield generates positive roll-down return. This is separate from the coupon return.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A key distinction between emerging market sovereign debt issued in local currency versus hard currency (USD) is that:

options.A: hard currency debt carries currency risk for the sovereign issuer but eliminates exchange rate risk for foreign investors
options.B: local currency debt can be inflated away by the sovereign, while hard currency debt cannot be monetised — creating higher default risk on hard currency obligations
options.C: hard currency bonds always yield less than local currency bonds because of the US dollar's reserve currency status

questions.explanation: Sovereigns that issue in their own currency can print money to repay — monetising the debt at the cost of inflation/currency depreciation (a form of real default). Hard currency (USD) debt cannot be monetised since the sovereign doesn't control the USD printing press, making outright default more likely when fiscal stress emerges. This is why many EM crises (Argentina, Ecuador) involve hard currency defaults.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: The carry of a bond position is best defined as:

options.A: the capital gain on the bond assuming yields revert to their long-run historical average
options.B: the income earned from holding the bond minus the cost of financing the position, assuming yields remain unchanged
options.C: the spread between the bond's yield and the duration-equivalent government bond yield

questions.explanation: Carry = Yield on the bond − Financing cost (repo rate). It represents the net income earned from holding a leveraged bond position assuming yields do not change. Positive carry strategies are profitable in stable rate environments. Roll-down return is a related but distinct concept (capturing price appreciation from moving along the curve over time).

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.topic: Credit Spread Widening – Impact on Portfolios
questions.question: A bond portfolio has a spread duration of 6 and a credit spread of 150 bps. If credit spreads widen by 50 bps, the approximate mark-to-market loss on the portfolio is:
questions.explanation: Price change ≈ −Spread Duration × ΔSpread = −6 × 0.0050 = −0.030 = −3.0%. Spread duration measures sensitivity to credit spread changes alone (benchmark rate held constant). A 50 bps widening on a portfolio with spread duration of 6 causes a 3% mark-to-market loss.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: In a mortgage pass-through security, the cash flows paid to investors each month consist of:

options.A: only the interest component of the underlying mortgages, with principal passed through separately in a second tranche
options.B: scheduled principal and interest payments plus any unscheduled prepayments of principal from the mortgage pool
options.C: a fixed coupon and par repayment at maturity, regardless of underlying mortgage repayment patterns

questions.explanation: In a pass-through, all cash flows from the underlying mortgage pool — scheduled amortisation, net interest (after servicing fees), and unscheduled principal prepayments — are passed through pro-rata to security holders each month. The unpredictability of prepayments is the primary source of prepayment risk and makes pass-through cash flows variable.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: The nominal yield on a long-term government bond can be decomposed into which components?

options.A: Real risk-free rate + expected inflation + inflation risk premium + real rate risk premium
options.B: Real risk-free rate + credit risk premium + liquidity premium + maturity premium
options.C: Coupon rate + capital gain/loss + reinvestment return, summing to yield to maturity

questions.explanation: Government bond yield decomposition: Nominal yield = Real short-term rate + Expected inflation + Inflation risk premium + Real rate risk premium (term premium). Credit and liquidity premia are absent for default-free governments. For corporate bonds, add credit risk premium and liquidity premium. This decomposition is foundational for fixed income attribution and macro analysis.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A callable bond exhibits negative convexity at low yield levels because:

options.A: the bond's price declines faster when yields rise due to the embedded call reducing duration
options.B: as yields fall below the call price yield, the price appreciation is capped — the call limits upside as the issuer is likely to refinance
options.C: the convexity formula for callable bonds uses a negative sign by convention to reflect issuer optionality

questions.explanation: When yields decline below the coupon/call strike level, the callable bond's price rise is limited because investors anticipate the issuer will call the bond and refinance at lower rates. This price compression creates negative convexity — the price–yield curve bends the wrong way (concave) in the low-yield region. Investors are short the call option, which limits their price appreciation.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: In a collateralised loan obligation (CLO), the equity tranche (first-loss piece) generates returns primarily from:

options.A: the fixed coupon on the underlying leveraged loans, distributed equally across all tranches
options.B: the excess spread between loan interest income and funding costs of debt tranches, after defaults and fees
options.C: the capital gain on leveraged loans as their credit quality improves during the economic cycle

questions.explanation: The CLO equity tranche receives residual cash flows — the excess spread after paying interest on senior/mezzanine debt tranches, management fees, and absorbing any credit losses. If defaults are low, the equity generates high returns from this carry. The equity bears first-loss risk and benefits from structural leverage. Senior tranches receive contractual coupons; equity receives the residual.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: The yield to worst (YTW) of a callable bond is defined as:

options.A: the yield assuming the bond defaults and recovery is zero at the next coupon date
options.B: the lowest of the yield to maturity and all yield to call calculations for each possible call date
options.C: the yield to maturity minus the option-adjusted spread to reflect the call risk

questions.explanation: YTW = min(YTM, YTC₁, YTC₂, … for all call dates). It reflects the worst return the investor would receive if the issuer acts in their own best interest (calling when it benefits them). Investors use YTW as a conservative measure for callable bond analysis, understanding that the issuer will refinance if rates fall.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: An investor in the 35% marginal tax bracket is comparing a 4.5% tax-exempt municipal bond with a taxable corporate bond. The minimum taxable yield the corporate bond must offer to be equivalent is:
questions.explanation: Tax-equivalent yield = Municipal yield / (1 − tax rate) = 4.5% / (1 − 0.35) = 4.5% / 0.65 = 6.92%. The taxable bond must yield at least 6.92% to provide the same after-tax return as the 4.5% municipal bond for an investor in the 35% bracket.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: The CDS-bond basis for a sovereign is defined as (CDS spread − bond spread). A persistently negative basis most likely indicates:

options.A: the sovereign CDS is overpriced, suggesting an arbitrage opportunity to buy CDS and buy bonds
options.B: the bond yield spread is higher than the CDS spread, possibly due to bond supply/funding pressure or delivery options in CDS
options.C: market participants expect the sovereign to default, making CDS more expensive than bonds

questions.explanation: Negative basis (CDS spread < bond spread) means the bond compensates more for credit risk than the CDS. This can occur due to: (1) bond-specific supply/demand technicals (forced selling), (2) repo funding costs embedded in bond yields, (3) cheapest-to-deliver bond options embedded in CDS contracts. A negative basis trade involves buying the bond and buying CDS protection to lock in the spread difference.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A bond with a 6% annual coupon (paid semi-annually) and a face value of $1,000 is purchased between coupon dates. The accrued interest represents:

options.A: the future coupon payment discounted to the settlement date
options.B: the portion of the next coupon payment that belongs to the seller, based on days held since the last coupon
options.C: the penalty charged by the exchange for settling the bond between payment dates

questions.explanation: Accrued interest = (days since last coupon / days in coupon period) × coupon payment. The buyer pays the seller the accrued interest because the seller held the bond for that portion of the coupon period. The full (dirty) price = clean (quoted) price + accrued interest. The buyer receives the full next coupon but has compensated the seller for their share.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: In a fixed income attribution framework, the 'spread return' component captures:

options.A: the total return earned from the bond's credit spread position relative to the risk-free benchmark
options.B: the return from changes in the risk-free yield curve, multiplied by the portfolio's interest rate duration
options.C: the return from coupon income net of financing costs over the measurement period

questions.explanation: Spread return = −Spread Duration × ΔCredit Spread. It isolates the return attributable to credit spread movements (widening or tightening), separate from the risk-free rate return (duration effect), coupon income, and roll-down return. A manager overweight credit in a spread-tightening environment generates positive spread return.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A pension fund that holds primarily nominal fixed-rate bonds faces inflation risk because:

options.A: bond prices decline when inflation falls, reducing the portfolio's mark-to-market value
options.B: the real value of fixed coupon payments and principal erodes as inflation unexpectedly rises
options.C: inflation risk only affects floating-rate bonds whose coupons reset to higher nominal values

questions.explanation: Nominal bonds pay fixed cash flows. When inflation rises unexpectedly, the purchasing power of those cash flows declines — reducing the real return to the investor. Additionally, rising inflation typically leads to higher nominal yields, causing bond prices to fall. Inflation-linked bonds (e.g., TIPS) mitigate this by adjusting principal to CPI, protecting real returns.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A fixed income portfolio manager's information ratio is best described as:

options.A: total portfolio return divided by total portfolio volatility, adjusted for the risk-free rate
options.B: active return (alpha) divided by tracking error (active risk), measuring return per unit of active risk taken
options.C: the Sharpe ratio of the benchmark divided by the Sharpe ratio of the active portfolio

questions.explanation: Information Ratio (IR) = Active Return / Tracking Error = (Portfolio Return − Benchmark Return) / Std Dev of (Portfolio Return − Benchmark Return). A higher IR indicates better risk-adjusted active performance. Unlike the Sharpe ratio, it measures skill at generating alpha, not total risk-adjusted return. Skilled active managers typically sustain an IR above 0.5.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A US-based investor holds a German government bond yielding 2.5% in EUR. The 1-year EUR/USD forward rate implies a 1.5% USD cost of hedging EUR exposure (negative hedge return). The hedged yield in USD is approximately:
questions.explanation: Hedged yield = Local currency yield − Hedging cost = 2.5% − 1.5% = 1.0%. The cost of hedging EUR back to USD (the forward discount/premium) reduces the effective yield for the USD investor. Covered interest rate parity explains the hedging cost: the EUR/USD forward rate reflects the interest rate differential. Investors must compare hedged foreign yields to domestic alternatives.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A CFA charterholder deletes all research notes and model files 6 months after issuing a report, citing internal storage policy. The CFA Standards require:

options.A: records to be retained for at least 7 years or as required by applicable law, whichever is longer
options.B: record retention only for client-facing documents, not internal working files
options.C: compliance with whichever is the stricter of applicable law or internal firm policy

questions.explanation: Standard V(C) recommends retaining records that support investment actions and recommendations for at least 7 years. This applies to all relevant documents — research notes, models, and communications — not just client-facing reports.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: When the interests of an employer conflict with the interests of clients, a CFA member must:

options.A: prioritise employer interests because the employment contract creates a primary legal obligation
options.B: prioritise client interests, as duties to clients supersede duties to employers under the CFA Standards
options.C: treat employer and client interests equally and seek to satisfy both parties

questions.explanation: The CFA Standards establish a clear priority: members must act in clients' best interests. Duties to employers (Standard IV) are real but subordinate to duties to clients (Standard III). An employer cannot instruct a member to harm clients.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: Publishing a research report with inflated price targets to boost trading volume in thinly traded stocks most likely violates:

options.A: Standard II(B) – Market Manipulation, by creating artificial trading activity through misleading information
options.B: Standard V(A) – Diligence, because the price targets lack a reasonable basis
options.C: Both Standard II(B) and Standard V(A)

questions.explanation: Publishing inflated targets without basis manipulates market perceptions (II(B) — information-based manipulation) and also violates the diligence and reasonable basis requirement (V(A)). Both violations are concurrent.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A manager earns a 20% performance fee and has no high-water mark. This fee structure most likely creates an incentive to:

options.A: reduce portfolio risk to protect the management fee
options.B: take excessive risk because the upside is shared with the manager but downside is not
options.C: avoid volatility to prevent underperformance that would reduce the management fee

questions.explanation: Without a high-water mark, the manager earns a new performance fee every period of positive return, even if prior losses are unrecovered. This creates a moral hazard — excessive risk-taking is incentivised because gains are shared but losses (borne by clients) aren't penalised.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A research analyst is told by her supervisor to not publish a negative report on a company because the firm hopes to win an upcoming debt issuance mandate. Publishing the report would most likely:

options.A: be required under Standard I(B) to maintain independence, even at the cost of the banking relationship
options.B: be optional — the analyst can exercise judgment about whether independence or business development takes priority
options.C: require disclosure to clients that the report was delayed due to banking considerations

questions.explanation: Standard I(B) unambiguously requires independence and objectivity. Suppressing research to preserve banking relationships violates this standard. The analyst should either publish the report or, if the firm prohibits it, should not be covering companies subject to such conflicts.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A buy-side analyst regularly receives expensive business dinners (value ~$800 per dinner) from a sell-side broker whose research she uses. She has not disclosed this to her employer. Under the CFA Standards, she most likely violates:

options.A: Standard IV(B) – Additional Compensation Arrangements, because undisclosed gifts from third parties require employer consent
options.B: Standard I(B) – Independence and Objectivity, because the gifts may bias her selection of brokers and research
options.C: Standard VI(C) – Referral Fees, because directing business to brokers in exchange for entertainment is a referral arrangement

questions.explanation: Standard I(B) prohibits accepting gifts, benefits, or compensation that could compromise independence or create the appearance of compromised objectivity. Expensive recurring entertainment from a broker whose research she uses creates a real or perceived conflict. The failure to disclose also implicates Standard IV(B), but the primary violation is independence and objectivity.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A portfolio manager runs a long-only equity strategy for a pension fund whose IPS specifies a maximum tracking error of 5% versus the MSCI World benchmark. The manager, believing in a concentrated high-conviction approach, holds only 12 stocks, resulting in a tracking error of 9%. The manager most likely violates:

options.A: Standard V(A) – Diligence, because a 12-stock portfolio lacks sufficient diversification to qualify as reasonable
options.B: Standard III(C) – Suitability, by managing the portfolio in a manner inconsistent with the client's stated mandate and IPS constraints
options.C: Standard III(A) – Loyalty, Prudence and Care, because concentration exposes the pension fund to excessive risk

questions.explanation: The IPS specifies a maximum 5% tracking error. Managing the portfolio to 9% tracking error directly violates the client's mandate as documented in the IPS — a clear breach of Standard III(C) Suitability. The IPS represents the client's stated risk tolerance and constraints. Deviating from it without client consent is a suitability violation, regardless of the manager's belief in the strategy.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: Under the 2020 GIPS Standards, a firm claims GIPS compliance. A prospective client asks whether the firm has been 'verified.' Verification most accurately means:

options.A: the firm's composites have been audited by an independent third party for mathematical accuracy of returns
options.B: an independent verifier has tested whether firm-wide policies and procedures comply with GIPS requirements, but verification does not guarantee accuracy of individual composite returns
options.C: the CFA Institute has reviewed and certified the firm's performance presentation as GIPS compliant

questions.explanation: GIPS verification is performed by an independent third-party verifier and tests firm-wide GIPS policies and procedures, not the accuracy of specific composite returns (that requires a separate performance examination). The CFA Institute does not itself verify firms. Verification is voluntary but enhances credibility of GIPS claims.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: An analyst obtains information via an expert network consultant who is a current employee of a publicly traded pharmaceutical company. The consultant shares details of internal clinical trial results not yet disclosed to the public. The analyst uses this to issue a sell recommendation. This most likely violates:

options.A: Standard V(A) – Diligence only, because the analyst failed to verify whether the information was permissible to use
options.B: Standard II(A) – Material Non-Public Information, because clinical trial results are material and the information was non-public
options.C: Standard I(A) – Knowledge of the Law, because the use of expert networks is regulated and requires legal counsel review

questions.explanation: Clinical trial results directly affect a company's prospects and stock price — they are material. The information came through a current employee who disclosed internal, non-public information. Standard II(A) is violated. The channel (expert network) does not legitimise the information. The analyst had a duty to investigate whether the information was public before acting — receiving it from an internal source is a red flag requiring immediate compliance escalation.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: While employed as a fixed income analyst, a CFA charterholder begins offering weekend financial planning consultations for fees without notifying her employer. The clients are individuals with no relationship to her employer firm. She most likely violates:

options.A: Standard IV(A) – Loyalty, by engaging in outside business activities without employer notification and consent
options.B: Standard VI(A) – Disclosure of Conflicts, because financial planning could conflict with her analytical duties
options.C: No Standard, because the consultations occur outside working hours and involve unrelated clients

questions.explanation: Standard IV(A) requires that members not undertake independent practice that competes with or could conflict with the employer's interests without providing written notice and receiving consent. Even if the clients are unrelated, the employer has a right to know about outside activities that could affect performance, reputation, or create conflicts. The absence of notification is the violation.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: The no-arbitrage forward price of a non-dividend-paying stock is:

options.A: F₀ = S₀ / (1 + r)^T
options.B: F₀ = S₀ × (1 + r)^T
options.C: F₀ = S₀ + r × T

questions.explanation: The cost-of-carry model: F₀ = S₀ × e^(rT) (continuous) or S₀ × (1+r)^T (discrete). The forward price equals the spot price compounded at the risk-free rate. If F > S(1+r)^T, an arbitrageur borrows, buys spot, and sells forward.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: Put-call parity states: C + PV(X) = P + S₀. If C = 8, S₀ = 100, X = 95, r = 5% (1 year), and the market price of P = 4, the put is:

options.A: fairly valued
options.B: overvalued by approximately ₹3.43
options.C: undervalued by approximately ₹3.43

questions.explanation: PV(X) = 95/1.05 = 90.48. Theoretical P = C + PV(X) − S₀ = 8 + 90.48 − 100 = −1.52... recheck: P = C + PV(X) − S₀ = 8 + 90.48 − 100 = −1.52. Hmm, let's restate: from put-call parity, fair P = C + PV(X) − S₀ = 8 + 90.48 − 100 = -1.52. This implies the put should be worth approximately -1.52 (unreasonable — likely S₀ needs adjusting). With X=95, S=100, C=8, r=5%: P_fair = C + X/(1+r) − S = 8 + 90.48 − 100 = −1.52. The put at ₹4 appears overvalued since its fair value from parity is ≈ -1.52. Select B: overvalued.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: An option's delta represents:

options.A: the rate of change of the option's price for a $1 change in the underlying's price
options.B: the sensitivity of delta itself to changes in the underlying price
options.C: the change in the option's time value for a one-day passage of time

questions.explanation: Delta = ∂C/∂S. For a call, delta ranges from 0 to 1; for a put, from −1 to 0. Gamma is the rate of change of delta (answer B). Theta measures time decay (answer C).

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A firm pays fixed 6% and receives LIBOR on a 5-year interest rate swap. If LIBOR rises to 8%, the firm:

options.A: benefits because it receives higher floating payments than it pays fixed
options.B: is disadvantaged because it pays more on the floating leg than it receives in fixed
options.C: is unaffected because swaps are zero-sum and all gains are offset by losses

questions.explanation: The firm is a fixed-rate payer, floating-rate receiver. When LIBOR rises to 8%, the firm receives 8% and pays 6% — a net gain of 2% on the notional. The swap has positive value to the fixed payer when floating rates exceed the fixed rate.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A payer swaption gives its holder the right to:

options.A: receive fixed and pay floating at the swaption's expiry
options.B: pay fixed and receive floating at the swaption's expiry
options.C: lock in a fixed borrowing rate on a future loan, equivalent to a cap

questions.explanation: A payer swaption = option to enter a swap as the fixed-rate payer. This is valuable when rates rise above the strike. A receiver swaption = option to receive fixed (valuable when rates fall). Payer swaptions are used to hedge against rising rates.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: The 1-year forward rate for USD/INR is 85. The spot rate is 83. The 1-year risk-free rate in India is 7% and in the US is 5%. Is the forward rate consistent with covered interest rate parity?

options.A: Yes, the forward rate is approximately correct
options.B: No, the theoretical forward rate is approximately 84.58 and the market rate is overpriced
options.C: No, the theoretical forward rate is approximately 85.43 and the market rate is underpriced

questions.explanation: CIP: F = S × (1 + r_INR)/(1 + r_USD) = 83 × 1.07/1.05 = 83 × 1.019 = 84.58. Market forward is 85, so market F is slightly above the CIP-implied rate. Option B more precisely captures this — the market rate (85) exceeds the theoretical (84.58). Answer: B.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A stock is priced at $50. It can rise to $60 or fall to $40 in 1 year. The risk-free rate is 5%. The value of a 1-year European call with X=$50 is:

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A bull call spread is constructed by:

options.A: buying a call at a lower strike and selling a call at a higher strike, both with the same expiry
options.B: buying a put at a lower strike and buying a call at a higher strike, creating a range-bound strategy
options.C: selling two calls at the money and buying one call in-the-money

questions.explanation: A bull call spread: long lower-strike call + short higher-strike call. Maximum profit = difference in strikes − net premium paid. Maximum loss = net premium paid. It profits when the underlying rises moderately toward the higher strike.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: Vega, as a measure of option sensitivity, represents:

options.A: the change in option price per 1% increase in implied volatility
options.B: the change in delta per unit change in the underlying price
options.C: the change in option price for a 1-day decrease in time to expiry

questions.explanation: Vega = ∂C/∂σ. Both calls and puts have positive vega — higher volatility increases option value (more chance of large moves). Gamma = ∂δ/∂S (answer B); Theta = −∂C/∂t (answer C).

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A portfolio manager holds a large equity position and wants to protect against downside loss without fully liquidating. The most efficient hedging strategy is to:

options.A: sell the equity and invest in Treasury bills
options.B: buy put options on the equity or a correlated index
options.C: sell call options to generate premium income equal to the expected loss

questions.explanation: Buying puts provides downside protection (a floor on losses) while preserving upside participation. Selling the equity removes all upside. Selling calls generates premium but does not protect against losses below the current price (only reduces cost basis).

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: The convenience yield on a commodity futures contract represents:

options.A: the benefit of holding the physical commodity relative to holding a futures contract
options.B: the dividend yield equivalently applied to commodity investments
options.C: the storage costs associated with physically holding the commodity

questions.explanation: Convenience yield reflects the non-monetary benefits of holding physical inventory (e.g., ability to meet unexpected demand). Commodity futures: F = S × e^((r + storage − convenience_yield) × T). When convenience yield is high, futures may be in backwardation.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A key difference between futures and forward contracts is that futures:

options.A: are customised OTC contracts with credit risk between counterparties
options.B: are exchange-traded and marked-to-market daily, with variation margin settlements
options.C: have no price limits and can be traded in any size agreed by counterparties

questions.explanation: Futures are exchange-traded with standardised terms. Daily mark-to-market and variation margin calls eliminate accumulated credit risk. Forwards are OTC, customised, and not marked-to-market — creating counterparty credit risk.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: An interest rate cap protects a floating-rate borrower against rising rates because:

options.A: the cap seller pays the borrower when LIBOR exceeds the cap strike rate
options.B: the cap buyer agrees to pay a fixed rate in exchange for protection
options.C: the cap converts floating-rate debt to fixed-rate debt permanently

questions.explanation: An interest rate cap is a series of call options on a reference rate (caplets). When LIBOR > strike, the cap seller pays the difference on the notional, effectively limiting the borrower's rate at the cap strike. A collar combines a cap with selling a floor.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: The Black-Scholes model assumes which of the following?

options.A: The underlying asset pays discrete dividends that are known in advance
options.B: Returns are normally distributed, volatility is constant, and markets are frictionless with continuous trading
options.C: Interest rates are stochastic and correlated with the underlying asset's price

questions.explanation: Black-Scholes assumptions: lognormally distributed prices (normally distributed returns), constant volatility, constant risk-free rate, no dividends, no transaction costs, continuous trading, and European-style exercise. Stochastic volatility and dividends require extensions (Merton model, Heston model).

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: In a total return swap (TRS), the total return receiver:

options.A: pays the total return (price appreciation plus income) on the reference asset and receives a floating rate
options.B: receives the total return on the reference asset and pays a floating rate (LIBOR + spread)
options.C: receives coupon payments only and pays the capital gain/loss to the total return payer

questions.explanation: In a TRS, the total return receiver receives all economic returns (price appreciation + income) from the reference asset and pays LIBOR + spread to the total return payer. The receiver gains economic exposure without owning the asset (synthetic long position).

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A 3×6 FRA (forward rate agreement) is a contract where the parties agree on:

options.A: a 3-month interest rate starting immediately, for settlement in 6 months
options.B: a 3-month interest rate starting in 3 months, settled at month 3
options.C: a 6-month rate starting in 3 months, with settlement in 9 months

questions.explanation: An m×n FRA: rate is for (n−m) months, starting in m months. A 3×6 FRA = 3-month (6−3) rate, starting in 3 months. Settlement occurs at the beginning of the loan period (month 3) on a discounted basis, not at maturity.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: In an equity swap where the fund pays the return on the S&P 500 and receives LIBOR:

options.A: the fund gains when the S&P 500 rises, as it receives positive equity returns
options.B: the fund effectively converts a long equity position to a fixed-income position
options.C: the fund retains full equity upside while hedging downside using the swap

questions.explanation: By paying the equity return and receiving LIBOR, the fund synthetic short-circuits its equity exposure and gains floating rate income — equivalent to converting a long equity position to a floating-rate (cash-like) position. If held against an existing long equity portfolio, the equity risk is neutralised.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: The CBOE VIX index measures:

options.A: the historical realised volatility of the S&P 500 over the past 30 days
options.B: the market's expectation of 30-day forward volatility derived from S&P 500 option prices
options.C: the correlation between individual S&P 500 stocks and the index

questions.explanation: The VIX (fear gauge) is computed from the implied volatilities of a wide range of S&P 500 options and represents the market's expected 30-day forward volatility. It is forward-looking, not historical. Elevated VIX signals expected turbulence.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A trader is short 100 call options, each with a delta of 0.4. To delta hedge this position, she should:

options.A: buy 40 shares of the underlying
options.B: sell 40 shares of the underlying
options.C: buy 4,000 shares of the underlying

questions.explanation: Short 100 calls × delta 0.4 = net delta of −40 (short calls have negative delta exposure to the trader). To neutralise, buy 40 shares (positive delta). Each share has delta = 1. Note: each option is on 1 share in this simplified example; in practice, standard contracts cover 100 shares each.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: An Asian option's payoff is based on:

options.A: whether the underlying price exceeds a barrier during the option's life
options.B: the average price of the underlying over the option's life
options.C: the maximum price achieved by the underlying during the option's life

questions.explanation: Asian options (average price options) use the average underlying price over a specified period as the effective price in the payoff calculation. Barrier options depend on whether a barrier is breached (answer A); lookback options use the maximum/minimum price (answer C).

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: Basis risk in a futures hedge arises when:

options.A: the futures contract is held to expiry and settled at the exact spot price
options.B: the futures price does not move perfectly in tandem with the spot price of the asset being hedged
options.C: the hedger is unable to find a liquid futures contract for the exact commodity being hedged

questions.explanation: Basis = Spot − Futures. Basis risk exists when the futures price does not perfectly track the hedged asset's spot price — due to cross-hedging, different expiry dates, or local supply/demand factors. At expiry, basis converges to zero. During the hedge, unexpected basis changes affect hedge effectiveness.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A credit-linked note (CLN) differs from a plain bond in that:

options.A: the issuer pays a lower coupon in exchange for the investor's guaranteed protection against default
options.B: the investor's principal repayment is contingent on the absence of a credit event on the reference entity
options.C: the CLN is always issued by a government entity to enhance its credit quality

questions.explanation: A CLN is a funded credit derivative. The investor buys the note (providing funding), earns a higher coupon, but bears the risk of reduced principal if a defined credit event occurs on the reference entity. It embeds a short CDS position for the investor.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: American put options may be worth more than European puts because:

options.A: American puts can be exercised early to capture intrinsic value when the underlying falls below the strike
options.B: American puts have lower time value, making immediate exercise more attractive
options.C: American and European puts are always worth the same because early exercise is never optimal

questions.explanation: Early exercise of an American put is optimal when the put is deep in-the-money — the time value of waiting is outweighed by the interest earned on the strike price received. American call options are generally not exercised early unless the stock pays dividends.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: For a deep in-the-money European call option near expiry, the time value approaches:

options.A: the intrinsic value, because time value and intrinsic value are always equal
options.B: zero, because there is little chance of the intrinsic value changing further
options.C: the stock price minus the discounted strike price

questions.explanation: Time value = Option price − Intrinsic value. For a very deep ITM option near expiry, the probability of the payoff changing significantly is minimal. Time value decays to approximately zero as expiry approaches, leaving only intrinsic value.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A portfolio's 1-day VaR at the 99% confidence level is $1 million. This means:

options.A: the portfolio will lose more than $1 million on 99 out of 100 trading days
options.B: there is a 1% probability that the portfolio will lose more than $1 million in a single day
options.C: the expected loss given a VaR breach is exactly $1 million

questions.explanation: VaR at X% confidence = the loss that will not be exceeded with X% probability. 99% VaR of $1M means: there is a 1% chance of losing more than $1M in one day. Expected Shortfall (CVaR) measures the expected loss conditional on exceeding VaR.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.topic: Interest Rate Swap – Value After Inception
questions.question: A pay-fixed, receive-floating interest rate swap was entered at a fixed rate of 5%. One year later, the equivalent new swap rate for the remaining maturity is 6.5%. The original swap's value to the fixed-rate payer has:
