options.C: continue coverage normally as long as she personally does not trade on the information

questions.explanation: An inadvertent breach of an information barrier does not change the nature of the information — the analyst now possesses MNPI. She must immediately notify compliance, add the stock to the restricted list, and cease publication of research. Continuing coverage or publishing reports while in possession of MNPI would violate Standard II(A).

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.topic: Standard V(B) – Quantitative Models and Disclosure
questions.question: A fixed income manager uses a proprietary duration model that systematically underestimates risk in non-linear instruments. She knows this limitation but does not disclose it to clients. She most likely violates:

options.A: Standard V(A) – Diligence, because using a flawed model without remediation lacks a reasonable basis
options.B: Standard V(B) – Communication with Clients, by omitting a known material limitation of the investment process
options.C: Both Standard V(A) and Standard V(B)

questions.explanation: Knowingly using a model with material limitations without correcting or disclosing them violates Standard V(A) (the recommendation lacks the full diligence required) and Standard V(B) (clients cannot properly evaluate the strategy without knowing its risk model's known shortcomings). Both violations are concurrent.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: Under the CFA Institute Asset Manager Code, firms must have a risk management framework that:

options.A: eliminates all investment risk in client portfolios consistent with capital preservation goals
options.B: independently identifies, measures, and manages investment risks and makes risk disclosures to clients
options.C: is reviewed by external auditors annually and benchmarked against industry peers

questions.explanation: The Asset Manager Code requires firms to have a robust, independent risk management process that identifies and monitors investment risks, operational risks, and compliance risks. Firms must disclose significant risks to clients. The Code does not require elimination of risk or mandate external review — it requires effective internal management and disclosure.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A junior analyst is asked by her manager to prepare marketing materials that materially overstate the firm's historical performance. She suspects the numbers are incorrect but prepares the materials anyway. She most likely violates:

options.A: No standard, because she was acting on instructions and lacked decision-making authority
options.B: Standard I(C) – Misrepresentation, because she knowingly participated in creating false materials
options.C: Standard IV(A) – Loyalty, because she should have refused her employer's instruction

questions.explanation: Standard I(C) prohibits members from making or causing others to make misrepresentations. Preparing materials you suspect contain false information constitutes participation in the misrepresentation, regardless of seniority. Members may not use 'I was following orders' as a defence — they must dissociate from violations.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A manager uses client commissions (soft dollars) to purchase a Bloomberg terminal that is used 60% for client research and 40% for internal administrative purposes. Under CFA Standards, the manager should:

options.A: not use soft dollars for the terminal at all because it has any non-research use
options.B: allocate the soft dollar credit appropriately — use soft dollars for the research portion and pay from firm funds for the administrative portion
options.C: use soft dollars for the entire terminal cost because research use exceeds 50%

questions.explanation: CFA guidance on soft dollars (aligned with SEC Section 28(e) principles) requires that client commissions only fund bona fide research services. When a product has mixed uses, the manager must reasonably allocate the research-related portion (60%) to soft dollars and pay for the non-research portion (40%) from the firm's own funds.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: An executive tips his broker with MNPI. The broker calls a hedge fund manager, who trades on it. The hedge fund manager then shares the tip with his co-investor, who also trades. Which parties have most likely violated Standard II(A)?

options.A: Only the original executive (tipper) and the hedge fund manager
options.B: The broker, the hedge fund manager, and the co-investor — all downstream tippees who traded on or transmitted MNPI
options.C: Only the co-investor, because the hedge fund manager was acting in the clients' best interest

questions.explanation: Standard II(A) violations extend through the tippee chain. The broker violated II(A) by transmitting MNPI. The hedge fund manager violated II(A) by trading. The co-investor violated II(A) by trading on a tip received from someone they should have known possessed MNPI. Intent to benefit clients does not provide an exemption.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.topic: Standard III(B) – Trade Allocation – Block Trades
questions.question: A manager places a block buy order for 100,000 shares across multiple client accounts. Only 60,000 shares are filled. Under Standard III(B), the 60,000 filled shares should be allocated:

options.A: entirely to the largest client accounts to minimise the operational burden of split allocations
options.B: pro-rata across all accounts included in the block order, based on order size
options.C: to whichever accounts the manager determines will benefit most from the position

questions.explanation: Standard III(B) requires fair dealing in trade allocation. When a block order is partially filled, the standard approach is pro-rata allocation across all participating accounts in proportion to their share of the total order. Discretionary allocation based on account size or manager preference creates unfairness among clients.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.topic: Standard V(A) – Investment in New Asset Classes
questions.question: A portfolio manager trained in traditional fixed income is asked to manage a new cryptocurrency fund with no prior experience in digital assets. Under Standard V(A), she should:

options.A: accept the mandate because all investment assets can be analysed using the same fundamental principles
options.B: decline or ensure she obtains sufficient competence through additional education or by engaging qualified support before managing the fund
options.C: accept the mandate and learn on the job, as practical experience is the best form of education

questions.explanation: Standard V(A) requires that members maintain adequate competence and only undertake activities for which they have sufficient knowledge and skill. Managing a new and specialised asset class without relevant expertise violates the diligence standard. The member should acquire the necessary knowledge — through study, training, or qualified assistance — before accepting responsibility.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: Under the 2020 GIPS Standards, which of the following is most accurate regarding return calculation methods?

options.A: Time-weighted returns (TWR) are required for all composites because they eliminate the impact of external cash flows controlled by the client
options.B: Money-weighted returns (MWR) are required for all composites because they reflect the actual investor experience
options.C: Both TWR and MWR are permissible; MWR is required or recommended when the firm controls the timing of external cash flows (e.g., private equity)

questions.explanation: The 2020 GIPS Standards permit both TWR and MWR. MWR is required for private equity composites (and recommended for real estate, infrastructure) where the manager controls capital calls and distributions — making MWR a better measure of manager skill. TWR is used for liquid strategies where cash flow timing is client-driven.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A municipal bond dealer's employee makes a personal political contribution to the treasurer of a state that subsequently awards the firm significant bond underwriting contracts. This arrangement most likely violates:

options.A: Standard I(B) – Independence and Objectivity, because political contributions to influence business awards compromise the integrity of the capital markets
options.B: Standard VI(A) – Disclosure of Conflicts, if the contribution was not disclosed to the employer
options.C: Both Standard I(B) and Standard VI(A)

questions.explanation: Political contributions made to win business (pay-to-play) compromise the integrity of the market and violate Standard I(B). The employee's failure to disclose the contribution and its connection to business awards to the employer also violates Standard VI(A). Both violations apply simultaneously.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: An analyst gives her employer two weeks' notice and, during that period, begins developing client pitch materials for her new employer on her personal laptop during evening hours. She most likely violates:

options.A: Standard IV(A) – Loyalty, because she is preparing to compete against her employer while still employed
options.B: No standard, because she is using personal time and personal equipment
options.C: Standard III(E) – Preservation of Confidentiality, only if she uses client names in the materials

questions.explanation: Standard IV(A) prohibits activities that harm the current employer's interests, including taking active steps to compete during the notice period. Preparing competitive materials while still employed, even on personal time, violates the duty of loyalty. Members may plan their departure but must not actively work against the employer's interests until they have formally departed.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A firm's compliance manual requires research analysts to obtain prior approval from the investment banking department before publishing reports on companies engaged in live banking transactions. This policy most likely violates:

options.A: Standard IV(C) – Responsibilities of Supervisors, if the compliance department fails to prevent it
options.B: Standard I(B) – Independence and Objectivity, because it gives the banking department veto power over research content
options.C: Standard V(B) – Communication with Clients, by delaying timely research publication

questions.explanation: Standard I(B) explicitly prohibits arrangements where banking, sales, or other revenue-generating departments can review or approve research before publication with the intent to influence content. Approval authority over research — even framed as a compliance review — destroys research independence if it enables banking interests to shape analytical conclusions.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: An analyst conducts interviews with former employees, suppliers, and customers of a company as part of her due diligence. She builds a picture that the company's flagship product will fail — information not publicly disclosed. Her analysis is based entirely on public and non-material non-public sources. Under the CFA Standards:

options.A: she must obtain management confirmation before publishing her negative conclusion
options.B: her investment conclusion is permissible under the mosaic theory as she assembled non-material pieces to reach a material insight
options.C: she has violated Standard II(A) because her conclusion constitutes material non-public information

questions.explanation: The mosaic theory protects analysis that reaches a material investment conclusion by synthesising multiple pieces of non-material, non-public (or public) information. The analyst's conclusion is her own work product — the materiality test applies to the inputs, not the output of analysis. She may publish, provided the source data is not independently MNPI.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A portfolio manager accepts a paid board directorship at a private company whose shares she also purchases for client accounts. She does not inform her employer. This situation most likely implicates:

options.A: Standard IV(B) – Additional Compensation only
options.B: Standard VI(A) – Disclosure of Conflicts, and potentially Standard II(A) if she receives MNPI through the board role
options.C: Standard III(A) – Loyalty, because accepting outside compensation is always a violation

questions.explanation: A board directorship creates two risks: (1) a conflict of interest between her role as director (loyalty to the company) and portfolio manager (loyalty to clients) — violating VI(A) if undisclosed, and (2) access to MNPI through board meetings — which, if traded on, would violate II(A). Both must be disclosed to the employer. IV(B) is also relevant for the compensation aspect.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A manager becomes aware of a class action lawsuit against a company held in client portfolios. Participating in the class action would recover a small amount for clients but requires significant administrative effort. The manager should:

options.A: decline participation in all class actions as an administrative distraction from portfolio management
options.B: evaluate participation in the class action as part of the duty to act in clients' best interests, considering recoverable value and costs
options.C: automatically participate in all class actions to maximise every possible recovery for clients

questions.explanation: Standard III(A) requires that managers act in clients' best interests, which includes exercising legal rights such as class action participation. The manager has a duty to evaluate whether participation is beneficial — considering the recovery amount, legal costs, and management time. Neither blanket refusal nor automatic participation satisfies the duty of prudent decision-making.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: Under GIPS, the temporary new account (TNA) provision for significant cash flows allows firms to:

options.A: permanently exclude portfolios with large cash flows from composite return calculations
options.B: temporarily remove a portfolio from a composite when an external cash flow is large enough to distort the manager's investment decisions, then re-include it once invested
options.C: use actual cash flows rather than estimated cash flows for sub-period return calculations

questions.explanation: The TNA provision (formerly the large external cash flow policy) allows firms to temporarily move a portfolio to a TNA when a significant cash flow arrives — so that the uninvested cash does not distort the composite return. Once funds are invested (per firm-defined policy), the portfolio is returned to the composite. This is a composite construction choice, not a permanent exclusion.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A client gifts a portfolio manager a luxury watch worth $8,000 to express gratitude for good performance. The manager should:

options.A: accept the watch as it is a gift from a client, not a broker, and does not create a conflict
options.B: disclose the gift to her employer and obtain written permission, and evaluate whether it impairs objectivity
options.C: accept the watch but disclose only if it exceeds the firm's stated gift threshold

questions.explanation: Standard I(B) and Standard IV(B) both require that gifts from clients be disclosed to the employer, regardless of the source (client or broker). A gift of $8,000 is substantial and may create an expectation of preferential treatment, potentially impairing the manager's objectivity in managing the client's portfolio. Employer approval must be sought.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.topic: Standard V(A) – Sector Rotation Without Basis
questions.question: A portfolio manager shifts the portfolio entirely out of healthcare into technology based on a conversation with a friend who 'has a feeling' tech will outperform. No formal research was conducted. This most likely violates:

options.A: Standard V(A) – Diligence and Reasonable Basis, because the allocation change lacks adequate research support
options.B: Standard III(C) – Suitability, because healthcare was appropriate and technology is not
options.C: Standard I(B) – Independence, because the decision was influenced by a third party

questions.explanation: Standard V(A) requires that investment decisions be supported by adequate research and analysis. Acting on informal conversation and intuition — without fundamental, technical, or quantitative support — fails the reasonable basis standard. The manager has a duty to conduct or obtain diligent research before making material portfolio changes.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.topic: Standard III(D) – GIPS and Performance Presentation
questions.question: A firm presents 15 years of composite performance but removes the three worst-performing years from the presentation, citing a 'change in strategy.' This most likely violates:

options.A: Standard III(D) – Performance Presentation, by omitting unfavourable results that would give an incomplete picture
options.B: Standard I(C) – Misrepresentation, only if the firm claims GIPS compliance
options.C: Standard V(A) – Diligence, because changing strategy without basis lacks a reasonable foundation

questions.explanation: Standard III(D) requires that performance be presented fairly, accurately, and completely. Retroactively removing poor performance periods — regardless of the stated rationale — creates a misleading impression of the strategy's risk-return history. GIPS requires all periods to be presented. This is cherry-picking and is a direct violation.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A management consultant working on a corporate restructuring shares details of an imminent asset sale with an analyst friend at a hedge fund. The analyst trades on the information. The management consultant most likely violates:

options.A: Standard II(A) by tipping material non-public information, even though he is not a CFA charterholder
options.B: No CFA Standard because he is not a CFA Institute member or candidate
options.C: Standard VI(A) – Disclosure of Conflicts, for not disclosing the client engagement

questions.explanation: CFA Standards apply only to CFA Institute members and candidates. The management consultant who is not a member or candidate is not subject to the CFA Standards (though he may violate securities law). The analyst — who is a CFA candidate or member — violates Standard II(A) by trading on the MNPI received.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: An equity analyst at a large investment firm wants to write paid investment commentary on a personal blog. To comply with Standard IV(A), she must:

options.A: immediately stop the activity because all outside investment activities are prohibited
options.B: notify her employer and obtain consent, as the activity may conflict with her employer's interests
options.C: proceed freely because personal blogging outside office hours does not fall under the employer's jurisdiction

questions.explanation: Standard IV(A) requires that members not engage in independent practice that competes with or conflicts with the employer's business without employer notification and consent. Paid investment commentary from a professional analyst could directly compete with the firm's research output and must be disclosed and approved before beginning.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A compliance officer implements a blackout period requiring all investment staff to abstain from trading in securities being actively researched or in the firm's recommended list. This policy is best characterised as:

options.A: overly restrictive and in conflict with Standard VI(B), which only requires client-first trading
options.B: consistent with Standard VI(B) and reflective of reasonable firm-level controls to prevent front-running and conflicts
options.C: a violation of Standard IV(A) because it restricts employees' personal property rights

questions.explanation: Blackout periods are explicitly endorsed in the CFA Standards as a best practice for implementing Standard VI(B). They prevent front-running and avoid the appearance of conflicts. Firms may impose restrictions beyond the minimum Standard VI(B) requirement, and such policies are consistent with, not contrary to, the Standards.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A client's IPS was last updated five years ago when she was 55 and planning to retire at 65. She is now 60 and has decided to retire at 62. Which of the following best describes the adviser's obligation?

options.A: The adviser should wait until retirement to update the IPS, as changes are only needed at major life transitions
options.B: The adviser should update the IPS immediately to reflect the shortened time horizon, increased liquidity needs, and revised risk tolerance
options.C: The adviser should maintain the original IPS but mentally adjust the portfolio without documentation

questions.explanation: Standard III(C) requires that suitability assessments reflect the client's current circumstances. An earlier retirement significantly changes the time horizon, liquidity requirements, and appropriate risk level. The IPS must be formally updated and documented. Managing against an outdated IPS exposes clients to unsuitable risk and violates the suitability standard.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: Under GIPS, when a composite strategy makes significant use of leverage or derivatives, the firm is required to:

options.A: exclude the composite from GIPS compliance because leverage makes performance non-comparable
options.B: disclose the use of leverage and derivatives and their potential impact on composite risk and return
options.C: present returns on both a leveraged and unleveraged basis for investor comparability

questions.explanation: GIPS does not prohibit leverage or derivatives, but requires firms to disclose their use and explain how they may affect the composite's risk and return profile. Investors must receive sufficient information to understand and evaluate the strategy. Dual presentation (leveraged and unleveraged) is not required but may be provided as supplemental information.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A portfolio manager knowingly uses an expected return assumption for equities that is 3% higher than her own internal estimate in client presentations to make the retirement projections appear more optimistic. This most likely violates:

options.A: Standard III(C) – Suitability, because overstated projections may lead to under-saving
options.B: Standard I(C) – Misrepresentation, by presenting knowingly false assumptions that create a misleading impression
options.C: Standard V(B) – Communication with Clients, by omitting the actual return assumption used in the model

questions.explanation: Using deliberately inflated return assumptions in client presentations — when the manager knows they are unrealistic — constitutes a knowing misrepresentation under Standard I(C). The manager is creating a false impression to influence client behaviour (potentially to retain their business). Standard V(B) is also implicated, but the primary violation is intentional misrepresentation.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A supervisor establishes a written compliance manual with clear trading policies but never trains staff on the policies, conducts no monitoring, and performs no testing. An analyst subsequently violates the trading policy. The supervisor most likely:

options.A: has satisfied Standard IV(C) because a written policy was established
options.B: has violated Standard IV(C) because establishing a policy without implementing, training, monitoring, and enforcing it is insufficient
options.C: is not responsible because the analyst was a trained professional who should have known the rules

questions.explanation: Standard IV(C) requires supervisors to establish and implement — not merely write — compliance systems. A policy that exists on paper but is never communicated, monitored, or enforced provides no real protection. The Standard requires supervisors to take reasonable steps to ensure the compliance system is understood and functioning.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A short-seller publishes a report containing partially fabricated claims about accounting fraud at a company, intending to drive the stock price down to profit on short positions. This most likely violates:

options.A: Standard V(A) – Diligence, because the report lacks a reasonable basis
options.B: Standard II(B) – Market Manipulation, by using false and misleading information to artificially depress the stock price
options.C: Standard I(C) – Misrepresentation, only if the report is signed by a CFA charterholder

questions.explanation: Standard II(B) prohibits both transaction-based and information-based manipulation. Publishing fabricated claims to move prices is a classic information-based manipulation. Standard I(C) and V(A) are also implicated, but the primary violation — deliberately creating an artificial price through misinformation — is Standard II(B).

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.topic: Standard III(A) – Voting Proxies for Conflicted Issuers
questions.question: A manager must vote proxies for a company where the manager's firm has a significant investment banking relationship. The manager should:

options.A: vote in favour of management recommendations to preserve the banking relationship
options.B: abstain from voting to avoid the conflict entirely
options.C: vote solely in clients' best interests, guided by a proxy voting policy, and disclose the conflict

questions.explanation: Standard III(A) requires proxy votes to be cast in clients' best interests, not the firm's commercial interests. Where conflicts exist, the manager must follow a disclosed, pre-established proxy voting policy — not management preference. Abstention may be acceptable in rare cases but is not a default solution to conflicts.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.topic: Standard V(B) – Risk Factor Disclosure in Complex Products
questions.question: A manager recommends a leveraged inverse ETF to a retail client as a 'long-term hedge.' She does not disclose that due to daily rebalancing and volatility decay, the product is designed for short-term trading and is likely to erode in value over long holding periods. She most likely violates:

options.A: Standard III(C) – Suitability, because the product is inappropriate for retail investors
options.B: Standard V(B) – Communication with Clients, by omitting the material structural characteristics of the product
options.C: Both Standard III(C) and Standard V(B)

questions.explanation: Recommending a leveraged inverse ETF as a long-term strategy — when it structurally degrades in value over time due to daily rebalancing — fails both suitability (III(C)) and communication (V(B)) standards. Clients must understand the product's structural risks and limitations to make informed decisions, and recommending it for long-term holding ignores its known design constraints.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A hedge fund manager structures trades through an offshore entity in a jurisdiction with no insider trading laws, using information that would constitute MNPI under the law of his home country. He conducts all analysis at home but executes through the offshore entity. This arrangement:

options.A: is permissible under Standard I(A) because no local law in the jurisdiction of execution is violated
options.B: violates Standard I(A) and Standard II(A) because members must comply with the stricter of applicable laws, which includes home country law, and the CFA Standards
options.C: is a matter for legal counsel only and falls outside the scope of the CFA Standards

questions.explanation: Standard I(A) requires compliance with the most stringent applicable law — which includes the manager's home country law even when execution occurs offshore. The CFA Standards also apply regardless of jurisdiction. Deliberately structuring around insider trading law via offshore execution while maintaining home-country operations is a transparent attempt to avoid legal and ethical obligations.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A firm offers premium clients same-day access to research while standard clients receive reports 48 hours later. Under Standard III(B), this practice is:

options.A: prohibited because all clients must receive research at exactly the same time
options.B: permissible if the service tiers are disclosed in advance and the differential is based on the services clients pay for, not on insider information advantages
options.C: a violation if any premium client trades on the research before standard clients receive it

questions.explanation: Standard III(B) requires fair dealing but not identical treatment. Tiered service levels are permissible if they are based on legitimate differentials in services purchased and are disclosed to all clients. A premium research tier available to paying clients — not based on MNPI — is consistent with fair dealing. The firm may not, however, give some clients a trading edge over others through the timing of material research.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.topic: Standard VI(A) – Personal Investments in Covered Companies
questions.question: An analyst who covers the pharmaceutical sector personally holds shares in a company she rates as a Buy. She must:

options.A: immediately sell her personal holding to eliminate the conflict completely
options.B: disclose the personal holding to her employer and in her research report, per Standard VI(A)
options.C: downgrade the company to Hold to eliminate the appearance of conflict

questions.explanation: Standard VI(A) requires disclosure of conflicts of interest — including personal holdings in covered companies — to both the employer and in research reports. The Standard does not require divestiture (though that may be required by employer policy). Changing the recommendation to avoid the appearance of conflict is itself a violation of I(B) independence.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.topic: Standard II(A) – Selective Disclosure and Reg FD
questions.question: A company CEO invites 10 favoured analysts to a private briefing where she discloses revised earnings guidance not yet in a public filing. A CFA charterholder among the attendees should:

options.A: immediately publish his updated model to distribute the information as widely as possible
options.B: contact compliance, treat the information as MNPI, and refrain from trading or publishing until the company publicly discloses it
options.C: share the revised guidance only with his firm's portfolio managers to ensure his employer benefits

questions.explanation: Selectively disclosed earnings guidance is MNPI. The analyst must not trade or publish research reflecting the non-public guidance until the company makes a public announcement (e.g., via 8-K or press release). Both sharing internally and publishing externally constitute violations until the information is public.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.topic: Standard I(B) – Gifts in Emerging Markets
questions.question: A CFA charterholder working in a country where gift-giving to clients is a deep-rooted cultural norm receives an expensive gift from a client to celebrate the Lunar New Year. Local business custom suggests refusal would be a significant insult. The charterholder should:

options.A: accept the gift without hesitation as cultural norms override the CFA Standards
options.B: refuse the gift entirely regardless of cultural context
options.C: consult compliance, consider the gift's value and whether it could impair objectivity, and disclose it — cultural context does not override the Standards but informs proportionate response

questions.explanation: Standard I(B) applies globally. Cultural norms are relevant context but do not suspend the Standard. The charterholder should assess the gift's materiality, disclose it to the employer, and determine whether it could impair independence. Token cultural gifts (e.g., small symbolic items) may be acceptable with disclosure; substantial gifts that create obligation are not, regardless of cultural setting.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A member discovers that her firm is submitting false trade confirmations to pension clients to conceal losses. She has escalated internally twice without resolution. Under Standard IV(A), the most appropriate next step is to:

options.A: continue raising concerns internally until the board level is reached
options.B: dissociate from the fraud; reporting to regulatory authorities may be required by law and is consistent with the Standards
options.C: resign quietly to avoid personal liability without taking further action

questions.explanation: When internal escalation fails to resolve illegal activity, Standard IV(A) requires dissociation from the conduct. Standard I(A) may also require regulatory reporting depending on local law. Resigning without action when clients are being harmed does not fulfil the member's obligations. Regulatory reporting is permissible and may be required.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.topic: Standard V(B) – Scenarios and Probability Disclosure
questions.question: An analyst presents a base case price target of $80 but does not mention a downside scenario of $30 that she has modelled with 30% probability. This most likely violates:

options.A: Standard V(B) – Communication with Clients, by omitting material downside information needed to evaluate the recommendation
options.B: Standard I(C) – Misrepresentation, only if the $80 target is fabricated
options.C: Standard V(A) – Diligence, because downside scenarios are optional in equity research

questions.explanation: Standard V(B) requires communications to include information material to the investment decision. A 30% probability downside scenario with a price target 62% below the base case is clearly material. Omitting it creates a one-sided, misleading picture of the investment's risk-return profile — clients cannot make informed decisions without this information.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A firm discontinues a fixed income composite because the last client in the strategy terminated the mandate. Under GIPS, the firm must:

options.A: remove the composite's performance history from all GIPS-compliant presentations immediately
options.B: retain the composite in the firm's list and maintain the historical performance record for at least five years after termination
options.C: continue to include the composite in performance presentations for all future periods as if it were still active

questions.explanation: GIPS requires that terminated composites remain on the firm's list of composites for at least five years following termination. Historical performance must be preserved and available. Removing it would allow firms to hide poor track records by simply closing composites — a practice the standard explicitly prevents.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A CFA charterholder posts on a professional social media platform that a competitor's fund manager 'is incompetent and has defrauded clients' without any factual basis. This most likely violates:

options.A: Standard I(D) – Misconduct, by engaging in conduct reflecting adversely on professional integrity through unfounded defamatory statements
options.B: Standard I(C) – Misrepresentation, because the statement misrepresents facts about a third party
options.C: Both Standard I(D) and Standard I(C)

questions.explanation: Making unfounded public accusations of fraud against a named competitor violates Standard I(D) (conduct reflecting adversely on professional integrity — using one's professional platform to defame) and Standard I(C) (making a false factual claim about a third party — misrepresentation). Both violations apply concurrently.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.topic: Standard III(B) – Trade Allocation and New Issues
questions.question: A firm's compliance policy prohibits allocating hot IPO shares to family members of portfolio managers. A manager's spouse opens an account at the firm and is allocated IPO shares in a hot deal. The manager was not involved in the allocation decision. The manager most likely:

options.A: has violated Standard VI(B) because the allocation benefited a family member
options.B: has not violated any standard because he was not involved in the allocation decision
options.C: should have disclosed the spousal account relationship to compliance to avoid even the appearance of a conflict, even if not personally involved

questions.explanation: Standard VI(A) requires disclosure of potential conflicts including family accounts at the same firm. The manager's failure to disclose the spousal account creates an undisclosed conflict, even if he was not involved in the specific allocation. Disclosure allows compliance to apply appropriate controls. The violation is failure to disclose, not the allocation itself.
